title: High power bragg grating based semiconductor laser
abstract: A laser including: a gain chip; an external cavity incorporating a Bragg grating; and a baseplate; wherein a first end of the gain chip has a high reflectivity facet forming a first end of the laser cavity; a second end of the gain chip has a low reflectivity facet; and a second part of the external cavity comprises a Bragg grating, supported by the baseplate, the temperature of the baseplate being maintained through a feedback loop; wherein the optical length of the external cavity is at least an order of magnitude greater than the optical length of the gain chip; wherein the Bragg grating is physically long and occupies a majority of the length of the external cavity and is apodized to control the sidemodes of the grating reflection.
claims:
Claims What is claimed is: 1. A laser comprising: a semiconductor gain chip (110); an external cavity (140); and a first thermally conductive baseplate (160); wherein a first end of the gain chip has a high reflectivity facet (120) forming a first end of the laser cavity; a second end of the gain chip has a low reflectivity facet (130), allowing light generated from the gain chip to be coupled with a first end of the external cavity; and a second part of the external cavity comprises a Bragg grating (180) forming a second end of the laser cavity; wherein the external cavity comprising the Bragg grating is supported by the first thermally conductive baseplate; wherein the optical length of the external cavity is at least an order of magnitude greater than the optical length of the gain chip; wherein the physical length of the Bragg grating is greater than 20 mm and occupies at least 75% of the physical length of the external cavity; and wherein the Bragg grating is apodized to control the sidemodes of the grating reflection. 2. The laser of claim 1, wherein the external cavity comprises an optical fiber. 3. The laser of claim 2, wherein the Bragg grating is a fiber Bragg grating (FBG) within the optical fiber. 4. The laser of claim 1, wherein the external cavity comprises a waveguide. 5. The laser of claim 4, wherein the Bragg grating is within the waveguide. 6. The laser of claim 1, wherein the optical length of the external cavity is about twenty times the optical length of the gain chip or longer. 7. The laser of claim 1, wherein the physical length of the gain chip is in the range of about 500 Î¼ÏÎ¹ to 3 mm, and the physical length of the external cavity is more than about 40 mm. 8. The laser of claim 3, wherein the end of the fiber that couples to the gain chip is lensed and anti -reflection coated to increase a coupling efficiency between the gain chip and the external cavity. 9. The laser of claim 1, wherein a lens is placed between the gain chip and the external cavity to increase a coupling efficiency between the gain chip and the external cavity. 10. The laser of claim 1, wherein the gain chip is modified to include a beam expander to increase a coupling efficiency between the gain chip and the external cavity. 11. The laser of claim 1, wherein the gain chip is attached to the first thermally conductive baseplate next to a first side of a trench (230) in the baseplate while the Bragg grating lies within a groove on a second side of the trench; wherein the temperature of the first thermally conductive baseplate being maintained through a feedback loop comprising a first thermo-electric cooler (TEC) and a first temperature sensor attached to the first thermally conductive baseplate; wherein heat generated by the gain chip creates a temperature variation along the length of the first thermally conductive baseplate; and 

 wherein the Bragg grating includes a variation in grating pitch along its length to counteract the temperature variation created by the gain chip, such that a desired reflection response from the Bragg grating is maintained. 12. The laser of claim 11, wherein the first thermally conductive baseplate geometry is optimized such that the temperature decreases linearly versus distance on the second side of the trench and the linear temperature variation of the Bragg grating is counteracted by a linear wavelength chirp in the Bragg grating, so that the overall effect is the desired reflection response from the Bragg grating. 13. The laser of claim 8, further comprising: at least a second thermally conductive baseplate (310) on which the gain chip is mounted, wherein the lensed end of the fiber is mounted on the second baseplate adjacent to the second end of the gain chip; and at least a second temperature sensor (330) placed next to the lensed end of the fiber; wherein the second temperature sensor provides feedback to a second TEC attached to the second thermally conductive baseplate, such that both the lensed end of the fiber and the rest of the fiber are kept at the same temperature. 14. The laser of claim 8, further comprising: at least a second thermally conductive baseplate (310) on which the gain chip is mounted, wherein the lensed end of the fiber is mounted on the second baseplate adjacent to the second end of the gain chip; wherein the fiber comprises a non-grating section (410) extending from a location within the first thermally conductive baseplate (420) to the lensed end of the fiber. 15. The laser of claim 14, wherein the wavelength of the laser is tuned by adjusting the temperature of the Bragg grating within the first thermally conductive baseplate. 16. The laser of claim 14, wherein a temperature of the first thermally conductive baseplate is not actively controlled, rather is left to vary with a temperature of an external package containing the laser. 17. The laser of claim 1, wherein the Bragg grating is apodized using a Gaussian amplitude profile. 18. The laser of claim 17, wherein a sinusoidal chirp is further applied to the Bragg grating. 19. The laser of claim 1, wherein an output power of the laser is taken from the first end of the gain chip. 20. The laser of claim 1, further comprising a first monitor configured to measure output power of the laser, and a second monitor configured to measure a back facet monitor (BFM) current representing light intensity from the high reflectivity facet of the gain chip, wherein a ratio of the BFM value to the output power is used provide a feedback signal for locking the laser to a specific position on the Bragg grating spectrum. 21. The laser of claim 20, wherein a fast feedback loop using the ratio of the BFM value to the output power is used to reduce the low frequency phase noise of the laser.
